flow 2: app -> pages -> repository / controllers -> apihandler -> mysql

flow 4: app -> pages -> repository / controllers -> apihandler -> controllers

the diagram represents a system architecture with various components such as the frontend application, database, models, controllers, and services.
communication flows between different components are depicted through arrows.
the system includes a frontend application, a backend with repositories/controllers, an api handler, a database, models, controllers, background jobs, real-time communication, and push notifications.
the system uses a combination of direct and api-based communication.
the architecture follows a layered design with clear separation of concerns.
utente -> app — type=message; label="compilazione campi"; explanation="user fills form fields"
app -> server — type=message; label="doregistration()"; explanation="application initiates registration process"
server -> database — type=message; label="salvataggio dati"; explanation="server saves data"
server -> database — type=message; label="conferma inserimento"; explanation="server confirms insertion"
server -> database — type=message; label="generateotp()"; explanation="server generates otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="salvataggio otp"; explanation="server saves otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="chiede otp salvato"; explanation="server requests saved otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="riceve otp salvato"; explanation="server receives saved otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="$check = checkotp()"; explanation="server checks saved otp"
server -> app — type=message; label="mail otp"; explanation="server sends otp via email"
server -> app — type=message; label="sendotp()"; explanation="server sends otp"
server -> app — type=message; label="[$check] : sendauthtoken()"; explanation="server sends authentication token if otp is valid"
flow 1: utente -> app -> server -> database (registration process)
flow 2: utente -> app -> server -> database (otp verification process)
this sequence diagram models the registration process for a user interacting with an application through a server and database.
the user fills out form fields, which are then processed by the application, which in turn communicates with the server to save the data and generate an otp.
the server sends the otp via email and waits for the user to enter it back into the application, which then verifies the otp with the server.
if the otp is valid, the server sends an authentication token to the application.
the main flow involves the registration process, while the secondary flow involves the otp verification process.
utente -> app — type=message; label="compilazione campi"; explanation="user inputs data into the application"
app -> server — type=message; label="$checku: _emailvalidate()"; explanation="application validates email"
app -> server — type=message; label="$checkp: _passwordvalidate()"; explanation="application validates password"
server -> app — type=message; label="[{$checku and $checkp}: dologin()]" explanation="server processes login request"
server -> database — type=message; label="richiesta passwrod associata alla mail"; explanation="server requests password associated with the email"
database -> server — type=message; label="invio password"; explanation="database sends password"
server -> app — type=message; label="$check = checkpassword()"; explanation="server checks password"
server -> app — type=message; label="[{$check} : generatetoken()]" explanation="server generates token"
app -> server — type=message; label="[{$check} : sendauthtoken()]" explanation="application sends authentication token"
server -> app — type=message; label="salvataggio authtoken()" explanation="server saves authentication token"
app -> utente — type=message; label="apertura schermata home"; explanation="application opens home screen for user"
flow 1: utente -> app -> server -> database -> server -> app -> server -> app -> server -> app -> utente
this sequence diagram models the login process in a software application.
the user inputs their credentials into the application.
the application validates the email and password.
if both validations succeed, the server processes the login request.
the server then requests the password associated with the email from the database.
the database sends the password back to the server.
the server checks the password against the one provided by the user.
if the password is correct, the server generates a token and sends it to the application.
the application saves the authentication token and opens the home screen for the user.
the main flow involves interactions between the user, application, server, and database.
utente -> app — type=message; label="richiesta psw dimenticata"; explanation="user requests password recovery"
app -> server — type=message; label="lostpswrequest()"; explanation="application sends request to server"
server -> database — type=message; label="check mail"; explanation="server checks email"
database -> server — type=message; label="$checkmail=risposta db"; explanation="database responds to server"
server -> app — type=message; label="[{$checkmail==f}]"; explanation="server checks email status"
server -> app — type=message; label="genera token recupero psw"; explanation="server generates password recovery token"
server -> app — type=message; label="salvataggio token recupero psw"; explanation="server saves recovery token"
server -> app — type=message; label="conferma salvataggio"; explanation="server confirms saving"
server -> app — type=message; label="invio nuova password & token recupero psw"; explanation="server sends new password and recovery token"
app -> utente — type=message; label="mail con link"; explanation="application sends email with link"
utente -> app — type=message; label="comunicazione cambio password avvenuta"; explanation="user communicates password change successful"

application initiates card loading

this diagram models a user interaction with a mobile application that fetches data from a server and database.
the main flow involves the user opening the homepage, which triggers the application to request and receive data from the server and database.
the application then displays the received data and continues to fetch more data as needed.
there is a condition for loading more cards if the remaining number of cards is less than half the total requested.
the diagram highlights the interaction between the user, application, server, and database, ensuring efficient data retrieval and display.

flow 1: utente 1 swipes card for utente 2 -> app utente 1 tracks object preference -> server saves preference -> server asks if there are matches -> server receives response -> server checks for matches -> server displays match screen -> app utente 1 notifies match and opens chat

flow 2: utente 2 swipes card for utente 1 -> app utente 2 tracks object preference -> server saves preference -> server asks if there are matches -> server receives response -> server checks for matches -> server displays match screen -> app utente 2 notifies match and opens chat
this diagram models a sequence of interactions between users and their apps, the server, and the database in a matchmaking scenario.
users swipe cards to indicate interest, which triggers the server to check for matches.
the server saves preferences and asks if there are matches, receiving responses and checking for matches again.
if matches are found, the server displays the match screen, and the user's app notifies them and opens a chat.
there are two distinct flows, one for each user swiping a card.
the condition

flow 1: utente1 -> app1 -> app2 -> server -> database — "user inputs message, application sends message, server saves message, application retrieves message, user receives message"
this diagram models the process of sending a message between two users through applications and a server.
the main flow involves a user inputting a message, which is then sent to the server for saving, and finally retrieved by the recipient user.
notifications are also sent to the recipient user upon message receipt.
the diagram highlights the interaction between the user, applications, server, and database in a messaging scenario.
